(天智天皇, *Tenji-tennō*; 626 – January 7, 672), known first as **Prince Katsuragi** (葛城皇子, *Katsuragi no Ōji*) and later as **Prince Nakano Ōe** (中大兄皇子, *Nakano Ōe no Ōji*) until his accession, was the 38th [emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") who reigned from 668 to 671. He was the son of [Emperor Jomei](/wiki/Emperor_Jomei "Emperor Jomei") and [Empress Kōgyoku](/wiki/Empress_K%C5%8Dgyoku "Empress Kōgyoku") (Empress Saimei), and his children included [Empress Jitō](/wiki/Empress_Jit%C5%8D "Empress Jitō"), [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei"), and [Emperor Kōbun](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dbun "Emperor Kōbun").

In the 3rd year of Saimei's reign (斉明天皇三年), the empress designated her son as her heir; and modern scholars construe this as meaning that this son would have received the succession (*senso*) after her death or abdication. Shortly after, she died, and Emperor Tenji could be said to have acceded to the throne (*sokui*).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Tenji is said to have compiled the first Japanese legal code known to modern historians. The [Ōmi Code](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Code "Ōmi Code"), consisting of 22 volumes, was promulgated in the last year of Tenji's reign.[[2]](#cite_note-Ponsonby-Fane,_Richard_1959_p._52-2) This legal codification is no longer extant, but it is said to have been refined in what is known as the [Asuka Kiyomihara *ritsu-ryō*](/wiki/Asuka_Kiyomihara_Code "Asuka Kiyomihara Code") of 689; and these are understood to have been a forerunner of the [Taihō *ritsu-ryō*](/wiki/Taih%C5%8D_Code "Taihō Code") of 701.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Tenji invaded Korea in an attempt to support a revival of Japan's ally [Paekche](/wiki/Paekche "Paekche"), one of Korea's Three Kingdoms, which had been conquered by the Korean kingdom of [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla") in 660. He was seriously defeated at the [Battle of Baekgang](/wiki/Battle_of_Baekgang "Battle of Baekgang") by the combined forces of Silla and [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") China.

Tenji undergoes his ceremonial confirmation as emperor. He had postponed formalities during the period that the mausoleum of his mother was being constructed, and when the work was finished he could delay no longer. Up until this time, although he had been *de facto* monarch, he had retained the title of Crown Prince.[[4]](#cite_note-titsingh54-4)

**Emperor Tenji** (天智天皇, *Tenji-tennō*; 626 – January 7, 672), known first as **Prince Katsuragi** (葛城皇子, *Katsuragi no Ōji*) and later as **Prince Nakano Ōe** (中大兄皇子, *Nakano Ōe no Ōji*) until his accession, was the 38th [emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") who reigned from 668 to 671. He was the son of [Emperor Jomei](/wiki/Emperor_Jomei "Emperor Jomei") and [Empress Kōgyoku](/wiki/Empress_K%C5%8Dgyoku "Empress Kōgyoku") (Empress Saimei), and his children included [Empress Jitō](/wiki/Empress_Jit%C5%8D "Empress Jitō"), [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei"), and [Emperor Kōbun](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dbun "Emperor Kōbun").

In 645, Tenji and [Fujiwara no Kamatari](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Kamatari "Fujiwara no Kamatari") defeated [Soga no Emishi](/wiki/Soga_no_Emishi "Soga no Emishi") and [Soga no Iruka](/wiki/Soga_no_Iruka "Soga no Iruka"). He established a new government and carried out political reforms. He then assumed real political power as the crown prince of both [Emperor Kōtoku](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dtoku "Emperor Kōtoku") and Empress Saimei. Despite the death of Empress Saimei, he did not accede to the throne for seven years, and came to the throne only after the relocation of the capital to [Ōmi](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_%C5%8Ctsu_Palace "Ōmi Ōtsu Palace") in 668. He created Japan's first family register, the *Kōgo Nenjaku*, and the first code of law, the [Ōmi Code](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Code "Ōmi Code").

## Traditional narrative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Traditional narrative")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Poem-by-Emperor-Tenchi-%28Tenji%29-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png/250px-Poem-by-Emperor-Tenchi-%28Tenji%29-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png)](/wiki/File:Poem-by-Emperor-Tenchi-(Tenji)-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png)

Poem by Emperor Tenji

Tenji was the son of [Emperor Jomei](/wiki/Emperor_Jomei "Emperor Jomei"), but was preceded as ruler by his mother [Empress Saimei](/wiki/Empress_Kogyoku "Empress Kogyoku").

Prior to his accession, he was known as Prince Naka-no-Ōe (中大兄皇子, *Naka-no-Ōe no Ōji*).

### Events of Tenji's life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Events of Tenji's life")]

As prince, Naka no Ōe played a crucial role in ending the near-total control the [Soga clan](/wiki/Soga_clan "Soga clan") had over the imperial family. In 644, seeing the Soga continue to gain power, he conspired with [Nakatomi no Kamatari](/wiki/Nakatomi_no_Kamatari "Nakatomi no Kamatari") and [Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro](/wiki/Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro "Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro") to assassinate [Soga no Iruka](/wiki/Soga_no_Iruka "Soga no Iruka") in what has come to be known as the [Isshi Incident](/wiki/Isshi_Incident "Isshi Incident"). Although the assassination did not go exactly as planned, Iruka was killed, and his father and predecessor, [Soga no Emishi](/wiki/Soga_no_Emishi "Soga no Emishi"), committed suicide soon after. Following the Isshi Incident, Iruka's adherents dispersed largely without a fight, and Naka no Ōe was named heir apparent. He also married the daughter of his ally Soga no Kurayamada, thus ensuring that a significant portion of the Soga clan's power was on his side.

### Events of Tenji's reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Events of Tenji's reign")]

Naka no Ōe reigned as Emperor Tenji from 661 to 672.

* **661**: In the 3rd year of Saimei's reign (斉明天皇三年), the empress designated her son as her heir; and modern scholars construe this as meaning that this son would have received the succession (*senso*) after her death or abdication. Shortly after, she died, and Emperor Tenji could be said to have acceded to the throne (*sokui*).[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* **662**: Tenji is said to have compiled the first Japanese legal code known to modern historians. The [Ōmi Code](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Code "Ōmi Code"), consisting of 22 volumes, was promulgated in the last year of Tenji's reign.[[2]](#cite_note-Ponsonby-Fane,_Richard_1959_p._52-2) This legal codification is no longer extant, but it is said to have been refined in what is known as the [Asuka Kiyomihara *ritsu-ryō*](/wiki/Asuka_Kiyomihara_Code "Asuka Kiyomihara Code") of 689; and these are understood to have been a forerunner of the [Taihō *ritsu-ryō*](/wiki/Taih%C5%8D_Code "Taihō Code") of 701.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* **663**: Tenji invaded Korea in an attempt to support a revival of Japan's ally [Paekche](/wiki/Paekche "Paekche"), one of Korea's Three Kingdoms, which had been conquered by the Korean kingdom of [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla") in 660. He was seriously defeated at the [Battle of Baekgang](/wiki/Battle_of_Baekgang "Battle of Baekgang") by the combined forces of Silla and [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") China.
* **668**: Tenji undergoes his ceremonial confirmation as emperor. He had postponed formalities during the period that the mausoleum of his mother was being constructed, and when the work was finished he could delay no longer. Up until this time, although he had been *de facto* monarch, he had retained the title of Crown Prince.[[4]](#cite_note-titsingh54-4)

Tenji was particularly active in improving the military institutions established during the [Taika Reforms](/wiki/Taika_Reform "Taika Reform").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Death of the emperor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death of the emperor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/TenchiTennoRyo.jpg/250px-TenchiTennoRyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:TenchiTennoRyo.jpg)

Memorial Shinto shrine and [Mausoleum of Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_Emperor_Tenji "Mausoleum of Emperor Tenji") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%BE%A1%E5%BB%9F%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3 "ja:御廟野古墳")]

Following his death in 672, there ensued a succession dispute between his fourteen children (many by different mothers). In the end, he was succeeded by his son, [Prince Ōtomo](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dbun "Emperor Kōbun"), also known as Emperor Kōbun, then by Tenji's brother [Prince Ōama](/wiki/Emperor_Tenmu "Emperor Tenmu"), also known as Emperor Tenmu. Almost one hundred years after Tenji's death, the throne passed to his grandson [Emperor Kōnin](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dnin "Emperor Kōnin").

:   **Post-Meiji chronology**

    * ***In the 10th year of [Tenji](/wiki/Tenji_(period) "Tenji (period)")***, in the 11th month (671): Emperor Tenji, in the 10th year of his reign (天智天皇十年), designated his son as his heir; and modern scholars construe this as meaning that the son would have received the succession (*senso*) after his father's death. Shortly thereafter, Emperor Kōbun is said to have acceded to the throne (*sokui*).[[6]](#cite_note-6) If this understanding were valid, then it would follow:

    :   * ***In the 1st year of [Kōbun](/wiki/K%C5%8Dbun_(period) "Kōbun (period)")*** (672): Emperor Kōbun, in the 1st year of his reign (弘文天皇元年), died; and his uncle Ōaomi*-shinnō* received the succession (*senso*) after the death of his nephew. Shortly thereafter, Emperor Tenmu could be said to have acceded to the throne (*sokui*).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

:   **Pre-Meiji chronology**
:   Prior to the 19th century, Ōtomo was understood to have been a mere interloper, a pretender, an anomaly; and therefore, if that commonly accepted understanding were to have been valid, then it would have followed:

    * ***In the 10th year of [Tenji](/wiki/Tenji_(period) "Tenji (period)")***, in the 11th month (671): Emperor Tenji, in the 10th year of his reign (天智天皇十年)), died; and despite any military confrontations which ensued, the brother of the dead sovereign would have received the succession (*senso*); and after a time, it would have been understood that Emperor Tenmu rightfully acceded to the throne (*sokui*).

The actual site of Tenji's grave is known.[[8]](#cite_note-kunaicho-8) This emperor is traditionally venerated at a memorial Shinto shrine (*misasagi*) at [Yamashina-ku, Kyoto](/wiki/Yamashina-ku,_Kyoto "Yamashina-ku, Kyoto").

The [Imperial Household Agency](/wiki/Imperial_Household_Agency "Imperial Household Agency") designates this location as Tenji's mausoleum. It is formally named *Yamashina no misasagi*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Poetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Poetry")]

The *[Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū")* includes poems attributed to emperors and empresses; and according to [Donald Keene](/wiki/Donald_Keene "Donald Keene"), evolving *Man'yōshū* studies have affected the interpretation of even simple narratives like "The Three Hills." The poem was long considered to be about two male hills in a quarrel over a female hill, but scholars now consider that Kagu and Miminashi might be female hills in love with the same male hill, Unebi.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This still-unresolved enigma in poetic form is said to have been composed by Emperor Tenji while he was still Crown Prince during the reign of Empress Saimei:

Such is love   since [the age of the gods](/wiki/The_Age_of_the_Gods "The Age of the Gods");  As it was thus   in the early days,  So people   strive for spouses   even now.[[11]](#cite_note-11) |

After his death, his wife, [Empress Yamato](/wiki/Empress_Yamato "Empress Yamato") wrote a song of longing about her husband.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### *Kugyo*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kugyo")]

The top court officials (公卿, *Kugyō*) during Emperor Tenji's reign included:

* *[Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")*: [Ōtomo *no Ōji*](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dbun "Emperor Kōbun") (大友皇子), 671–672.[[14]](#cite_note-b268-14)
* *Naishin* (内臣): [Fujiwara no Kamatari](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Kamatari "Fujiwara no Kamatari") (藤原鎌足) (614–669), 645–669.[[14]](#cite_note-b268-14)

Prince Ōtomo (Ōtomo*-shinnō*) was the favorite son of Emperor Tenji; and he was also the first to be accorded the title of *Daijō-daijin.*[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Non*-nengō* period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non-nengō period")]

The years of Tenji's reign are not linked by scholars to any era or *nengō*.[[4]](#cite_note-titsingh54-4) The Taika era innovation of naming time periods – *nengō* – languished until Mommu reasserted an imperial right by proclaiming the commencement of Taihō in 701 (aside from the momentary proclamation of the [Shuchō](/wiki/Shuch%C5%8D "Shuchō") era under Emperor Tenmu in 686).

* See [Japanese era name – *"Non-nengo periods"*](/wiki/Japanese_era_name "Japanese era name")
* See [Tenji period](/wiki/Tenji_period "Tenji period") (661).

In this context, Brown and Ishida's translation of *Gukanshō* offers an explanation about the years of Empress Jitō's reign which muddies a sense of easy clarity in the pre-Taiho time-frame:

:   :   "The eras that fell in this reign were: (1) the remaining seven years of Shuchō [(686+7=692?)]; and (2) Taika, which was four years long [695–698]. (The first year of this era was *kinoto-hitsuji* [695].)  ... In the third year of the Taka era [697], Empress Jitō yielded the throne to the Crown Prince."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Mausoleum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emperor_Tenji&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mausoleum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Gobyono_Kofun%2C_haisho-1.jpg/250px-Gobyono_Kofun%2C_haisho-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gobyono_Kofun,_haisho-1.jpg)

[Mausoleum of Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_Emperor_Tenji "Mausoleum of Emperor Tenji") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%BE%A1%E5%BB%9F%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3 "ja:御廟野古墳")] is a [Kofun](/wiki/Kofun "Kofun") in that is the traditional burial site of Emperor Tenji. Specifically, it is an [Octagonal Kofun](/wiki/Octagonal_Kofun "Octagonal Kofun") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E8%A7%92%E5%A2%B3 "ja:八角墳")].[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

The [Imperial Household Agency](/wiki/Imperial_Household_Agency "Imperial Household Agency") has limited access by the public out of respect for Emperor Tenji who they claim is buried there.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)
